svg.text: Median of Medians — Worst-Case O(n) Pivot
2. Sort each group, take its median. (O(n), sort of 5 is O(1).)
3. Recursively quickselect the median of those ⌈n/5⌉ medians. (T(n/5))
4. Use that as pivot; partition input; recurse into one side. (T(7n/10) + n)
Why 7n/10? The pivot beats at least 3 of the 5 elements in at least half of the groups
= 3n/10 elements, so the larger side holds ≤ 7n/10.
• Groups of 3: T(n/3) + T(2n/3) + n → Θ(n log n). NOT linear. 5 is the smallest group size that works.
• Groups of 7: linear too, smaller constant; rarely seen because 5 is the textbook standard.
• Floyd–Rivest (1975): samples ~√n elements, picks two bounds from the sample, partitions 3-way.
Expected comparisons: n + k + o(n) — tightest known constant.
• Introselect (STL nth_element): quickselect with random pivot, fall back to median-of-medians
if depth exceeds 2·log n. Gives O(n) worst-case with quickselect's average constants.
Median-of-medians has large constants — use only when worst-case matters.
